text.title: Feedback Management Pipeline
text.summary: Defines the six-stage feedback pipeline (Collect → Categorize → Analyze → Prioritize → Act → Close Loop), feedback source quality matrix, and fixed taxonomy rules.

list.item: Support tickets: high volume, high quality (real blocking issues).
In-app feedback: medium volume, medium quality (prompted, context-rich).
App store reviews: medium volume, mixed quality (public, visible to prospects).
Social media: variable volume, variable quality (unsolicited, context-poor).
Sales calls: low volume, high quality (prospect-level, high intent).
NPS surveys: medium volume, medium quality (prompted, structured).
User interviews: low volume, very high quality (solicited, in-depth).
Analytics: high volume, high quality for behavioral patterns.

rule.statement: Pin the taxonomy in every triage prompt as a closed enum and validate all agent output against it. Never allow the agent to invent new tags.
rule.rationale: Taxonomy drift makes aggregation impossible; "billing" on Monday becomes "pricing" on Friday, destroying trend analysis.

list.item: Type: bug | request | enhancement | confusion | praise | complaint.
Topic: onboarding | core-A | core-B | billing | integrations | performance | other.
Segment: free | solo | pro | geek | unknown.

rule.statement: Always store the original verbatim alongside tags. Never let the agent paraphrase the user's words — re-tagging later is impossible without the source string.
rule.rationale: Close-loop emails that paraphrase the user's original complaint sound robotic and erode trust; the exact quote is irreplaceable.

rule.statement: Stage 1 — Centralize: maintain one canonical feedback store (Productboard, Canny, Linear, or Notion). Auto-import from all active sources; no channel-exclusive silos.
rule.rationale: Feedback scattered across channels prevents pattern detection and creates unequal attention between loud (social) and quiet (support ticket) channels.

rule.statement: Stage 2 — Triage: categorize each item individually, not in batch. Require a confidence field; route items below 0.7 confidence to a human queue.
rule.rationale: Batch triage hides per-item confidence and causes the model to over-cluster; individual processing surfaces edge cases.

rule.statement: Stage 3 — Aggregate: weight aggregations by ARR / retention risk / segment, not raw mention count. A silent enterprise account outweighs 10 vocal free-tier users.
rule.rationale: Mention-count aggregation reproduces the squeaky-wheel bias; segment weighting aligns feedback priority with business impact.

rule.statement: Stage 4 — Prioritize: link feedback themes to backlog items using RICE or equivalent; record "build / won't do / need more info / already planned" decision for every significant theme.
rule.rationale: Without a link between feedback and backlog, themes are acknowledged but never acted on; the explicit four-option decision forces a documented choice.

rule.statement: Stage 5 — Act: when a decision is made, assign an owner and a target sprint or explicit deferral date. Themes without owners are never shipped.
rule.rationale: Ownership without a deadline produces indefinite deferral; the sprint assignment is the commitment mechanism.

rule.statement: Stage 6 — Close Loop: notify requesters when their feedback ships, is declined (with honest reason), or needs more information. Keep close-loop emails as drafts for human review — never auto-send.
rule.rationale: Auto-sent close-loop emails that misquote or misdate the user's original feedback destroy trust faster than silence.

text.reference: Bash script: pipes feedback items to Claude Haiku triage agent, emits JSONL with type/topic/segment/sentiment/confidence per item.
Feedback log template with by-category summary, top requests, and trends section.
